messages.content: 你好！需要我帮你做什么？如果你愿意，可以告诉我你想聊的话题或要解决的问题（比如学习、写作、翻译、编程、旅行规划等）。

写一段主题为Multimodal Chain-of-Thought的related work，用在我的文章中，思路从文本的COT(Chain-of-thought prompting elicits reasoning in large language models) 引入快速过渡到视觉COT（一些数据集Visual-COT），分别简短介绍thinking about images（Vision-R1, R1-OneVision）和借助tool调用的thinking with images（Visual sketchpad，deepeyes），以及一些统一生成理解的工作（Chameleo, MVoT， Bage）
注意引用我给出的工作，可以参考以下两段但不能用其中的语句：
Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting was first shown to elicit step-by-step reasoning in LLMs by
supplying a few worked examples that include intermediate rationales [Feng et al., 2023, Zhang et al.,
2024a, Wei et al., 2023]. Recent extensions of CoT to multimodal settings embed visual evidence
directly into the reasoning trajectory. ICoT [Zhang et al., 2024b] interleaves attention-selected
image crops with text tokens, yielding significant VQA gains, while Visual CoT [Shao et al., 2024a]
supplies 438 k bounding-box-grounded rationales to train VLMs that emit explicit visual tokens and
improve spatial grounding. Recent works [Hu et al., 2024, Zhou et al., 2024, Yang et al., 2025, Gao
et al., 2024, Wu et al., 2025, Chern et al., 2025, Fang et al., 2025, Cheng et al., 2025a, Su et al., 2025]
further leverage external tools to supply visual cues that enrich multimodal CoT reasoning..
Recent works [Chen et al., 2025, Wang et al., 2025] like Chameleon [Team, 2025, Chern et al., 2024]
trains a unified token-based model that can emit arbitrary sequences of text and image tokens, but
at the cost of large-scale pixel-level supervision and heavier decoding. MVoT [Li et al., 2025a]
further trains a unified model to directly produce image and text interleaving trajectories, but absent
of reasoning thoughts. In contrast, our Mirage framework differs by emitting compact latent visual
tokens rather than real image patches or pixels, avoiding heavy image generation while still allowing
fully interleaved visual–text reasoning.
  
Chain-of-Thought (CoT) [41] improves LLM’s reasoning by decomposing complex problems into intermediate
steps. Recent works [14, 22, 38, 46, 48] have extended
this paradigm to multi-modal settings, with methods like
Vision-R1 [16] and Visual-RFT [28] training MLLMs for
step-by-step reasoning. However, their reasoning are forced to verbalize visual concepts in the linguistic domain, which
hinders their performance on vision-intensive tasks.
Existing efforts to enable “think with images” fall into
two categories. Tool-driven methods [29, 42, 53] equip
MLLMs with predefined external utilities for actions like
cropping, drawing, or marking regions. Visual Sketchpad [15], for instance, generates code to produce auxiliary visual aids, while DeepEyes [54] generates bounding
boxes and then employs zoom-in tools to crop images for
better reasoning. While effective to an extent, these methods depend on predefined tool-kits with fixed action spaces,
limiting flexibility, and failing to cultivate an intrinsic reasoning faculty. A second direction pursues unified foundation models like Janus-Pro [5] and Bagel [8], which interleave text and image generation within reasoning chains.
However, this paradigm is hampered by the prohibitive cost
of pixel-level synthesis. Moreover, their understanding is
bounded by their generative fidelity, meaning they cannot
reason about what they cannot draw. Their effectiveness has
also been shown mainly in specific domains such as navigation and maze solving [20, 45], raising concerns about
their generalizability. In contrast, SkiLa natively generates
intrinsic visual thoughts, eliminating dependencies on external tools or costly pixel-level generation pipelines

### Related Work: Multimodal Chain-of-Thought

Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting was originally introduced in the language domain to enhance large language models’ multi-step reasoning by inducing explicit intermediate inference steps, as exemplified by *Chain-of-thought prompting elicits reasoning in large language models*. Building on this idea, recent research has extended CoT from purely textual rationales to multimodal reasoning, where the model’s “thought process” is expected to incorporate visual evidence rather than only describing it post hoc. Along this line, several datasets and benchmarks (e.g., **Visual-COT**) have been proposed to support training and evaluation of vision-language models that produce stepwise rationales grounded in images.

A first branch can be summarized as **thinking about images**, where models still express their reasoning primarily in text while conditioning on visual inputs. Representative works such as **Vision-R1** and **R1-OneVision** aim to strengthen step-by-step multimodal reasoning ability via training recipes inspired by reinforcement learning or reasoning-centric supervision, improving the model’s capacity to derive structured conclusions from visual scenes. However, these approaches typically keep the reasoning trace in the linguistic space, which may be suboptimal when fine-grained spatial or appearance cues must be manipulated during inference.

A second branch emphasizes **thinking with images**, i.e., enabling the reasoning trajectory to actively interact with visual content through intermediate visual operations or external tools. In this category, **Visual Sketchpad** allows models to generate executable programs (e.g., drawing or annotating) to construct auxiliary visual artifacts that support subsequent reasoning steps. **DeepEyes** further leverages region proposal and zoom-in/cropping style operations, enabling iterative refinement over image regions to facilitate more precise visual grounding. While effective, tool-based paradigms often rely on predefined action spaces and hand-designed toolchains, which can limit generality across tasks and domains.

Beyond tool augmentation, another emerging direction studies **unified generation-and-understanding** frameworks that interleave text and image representations within a single model. **Chameleon** explores a unified token-based interface that can produce both textual and visual outputs in a single sequence, enabling flexible multimodal generation patterns. **MVoT** similarly targets unified interleaving of modalities to support integrated trajectories spanning perception and generation. **Bagel** further advances this line by strengthening the joint modeling of multimodal content within a unified framework. Although these methods offer an appealing route toward tightly coupled multimodal reasoning, they may introduce additional training and decoding costs when high-fidelity image synthesis is required, and their reasoning effectiveness can be influenced by the quality and controllability of the generated visual tokens.
写的不错，但太多了，每个工作用一句话概括就可以

Chain-of-Thought（CoT）最初在文本场景中被提出，通过显式中间推理步骤提升LLM的复杂推理能力（*Chain-of-thought prompting elicits reasoning in large language models*），随后研究快速将其扩展到多模态推理，并出现了用于训练/评测视觉推理链的数据集（如 **Visual-COT**）。在“**thinking about images**”路线上，**Vision-R1** 通过强化式训练范式提升MLLM的逐步视觉推理能力，**R1-OneVision** 则进一步将该类推理训练扩展到更通用的视觉任务与数据覆盖。与之相对，“**thinking with images**”强调借助外部工具在推理过程中显式操作视觉证据：**Visual Sketchpad** 让模型生成可执行绘制/标注的程序以构造中间视觉草图，**DeepEyes** 通过生成框选区域并调用缩放裁剪等工具实现迭代式的精细视觉定位与推理。最后，统一生成-理解方向尝试在单模型内完成文本与图像的交错建模：**Chameleon** 采用统一token序列来混合生成文本与图像，**MVoT** 学习产生交错的多模态轨迹以连接视觉与语言过程，**Bagel** 则进一步强化统一框架下的多模态生成与理解能力以支持更通用的视觉语言任务。

Chain-of-Thought (CoT) was first introduced in the text-only setting to elicit step-by-step reasoning in LLMs (*Chain-of-thought prompting elicits reasoning in large language models*), and it was soon extended to multimodal reasoning with dedicated resources such as **Visual-COT**. In the line of *thinking about images*, **Vision-R1** strengthens MLLMs’ multi-step visual reasoning via reasoning-centric training (e.g., RL-style optimization), while **R1-OneVision** scales this paradigm toward broader vision tasks and data coverage. In contrast, *thinking with images* relies on tool use during reasoning: **Visual Sketchpad** generates executable drawing/annotation programs to create intermediate visual aids, and **DeepEyes** predicts regions and invokes zoom/crop tools for iterative, fine-grained grounding. Finally, unified generation-and-understanding models interleave modalities within a single sequence: **Chameleon** adopts a unified token interface that can emit both text and image outputs, **MVoT** learns to produce interleaved multimodal trajectories, and **Bagel** further advances unified multimodal modeling for more general vision-language capabilities.
